title: Top Five Movie Soundtracks
format: Sean and Rob Harvilla argue about what actually makes a soundtrack great, then each run a five-deep countdown, before Sean talks to director John Maggio about his Robert Stigwood documentary Mr. Saturday Night.

films.title: Wayne's World
films.note: Rob's favorite music-movie moment (Bohemian Rhapsody in the car), though he can't name another song from the soundtrack besides Crucial Taunt's Ballroom Blitz cover.

films.title: Ghostbusters
films.note: The first soundtrack that ever hit Rob, via a cassette tape of Ray Parker Jr.'s title song when he was six.

films.title: The Breakfast Club
films.note: Sean's example of the one-song-does-it-all soundtrack, via Don't You (Forget About Me).

films.title: Ferris Bueller's Day Off
films.note: Sean's platonic ideal: the art-museum scene set to an instrumental Smiths cover permanently rewrote the song for him.

films.title: The Royal Tenenbaums
films.note: Rob on Gwyneth Paltrow's bus entrance to Nico's These Days recontextualizing a song he thought he already knew.

films.title: Juno
films.note: Rob tears up every time at the hospital scene set to Cat Power's cover of Sea of Love.

films.title: Palm Springs
films.note: Both hosts' favorite movie of 2020; Rob on John Cale's Barracuda landing like someone reading his mind.

segments.heading: Rob Harvilla's Top Five Movie Soundtracks
segments.subject: Movie soundtracks

films.title: Purple Rain
films.note: No. 1. Rob's New Testament to the Harder They Come's Old Testament: Let's Go Crazy, When Doves Cry, Darling Nikki, staged in the film as a heel turn the crowd hates.

films.title: The Harder They Come
films.note: No. 2. The reggae soundtrack's gateway drug, from Many Rivers to Cross to the title song; Rob thinks the record is better than the movie, and the movie is fine.

films.title: Once
films.note: No. 3. The Irish busker film with Glen Hansard and Markéta Irglová; their Oscar winner Falling Slowly anchors a soundtrack Rob calls more human than Garden State's.

films.title: Waiting to Exhale
films.note: No. 4. Babyface-orchestrated murderer's row of 90s R&B: Whitney Houston, Toni Braxton's Let It Flow, Mary J. Blige's Not Gon' Cry, Brandy, Aretha, Patti LaBelle. Rob picks it over The Bodyguard despite I Will Always Love You being the bigger song.

films.title: Angus
films.note: No. 5. Rob's dark-horse pick: a scruffy pop-punk soundtrack (Green Day, Weezer's Say It Ain't So, Ash, the Psychedelic Furs' Richard Butler covering his own Am I Wrong under the band name Love Spit Love) for a small bully movie with James Van Der Beek's first credit, plus George C. Scott and, he says, Rita Moreno.

segments.heading: Sean Fennessey's Top Five Movie Soundtracks
segments.subject: Movie soundtracks

films.title: Super Fly
films.note: No. 1. Curtis Mayfield's score-as-character album (Freddie's Dead, Give Me Your Love, Pusherman); Sean picked it before learning critic Greg Tate, who championed the film's Criterion release, had just died.

films.title: Goodfellas
films.note: No. 2. Scorsese's needle drops (Rags to Riches by Tony Bennett, the Layla piano coda) chart the decades and shepherd Henry Hill's rise and fall.

films.title: Kill Bill: Vol. 1
films.note: No. 3. Tarantino's most unusual soundtrack: almost entirely pre-existing film scores and TV themes, not pop songs, including the Green Hornet theme.

films.title: Kill Bill: Vol. 2
films.note: No. 3 (paired with Vol. 1). Same collage approach, drawing on blaxploitation cues like Isaac Hayes's Truck Turner and spaghetti-western scores.

films.title: American Graffiti
films.note: No. 4. George Lucas's teenage-cruising soundtrack of late-50s/early-60s pop; Sean calls it a clear influence on Licorice Pizza.

films.title: Juice
films.note: No. 5. Ernest Dickerson's hip-hop soundtrack anchored by Eric B. & Rakim's title track and Cypress Hill's How I Could Just Kill a Man; Tupac Shakur (Bishop) doesn't appear on it because he wasn't signed to the label.

segments.heading: John Maggio on Mr. Saturday Night

films.title: Mr. Saturday Night
films.note: Maggio's HBO documentary on Robert Stigwood, the manager and producer who paired the Bee Gees to Saturday Night Fever and helped invent the movie soundtrack. Built almost entirely from archival footage; no talking-head interviews.

films.title: Saturday Night Fever
films.note: Stigwood's biggest gamble, made for a fraction of what it earned; Maggio calls it one of the darker American films once you look past the dance scenes.

films.title: Grease
films.note: The other Stigwood-Travolta smash from the same run.

films.title: Jesus Christ Superstar
films.note: One of the stage productions Stigwood turned into a film.

films.title: Tommy
films.note: Another Stigwood stage-to-screen production, from his run managing the Who.

films.title: Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band
films.note: Stigwood's big swing that missed, mentioned as the gamble that didn't pay off.

films.title: Shaft
films.note: Gordon Parks's biggest commercial hit, brought up because Maggio's prior documentary covered Parks and drew him toward this project.

films.title: Elvis
films.note: The Baz Luhrmann film producer Kevin McCormick (a key interview source) was shooting with Tom Hanks in Australia when Hanks announced his COVID diagnosis.

films.title: Val
films.note: Maggio's answer to the last-great-thing-he's-seen question: the Val Kilmer documentary, messy but affecting.

films.title: The Rescue
films.note: Sean recommends it back to Maggio as the one to see next.

films: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood

Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band.title: Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood
